summary: Gruber analyzes Snow Leopard's adoption rate among Daring Fireball readers using Google Analytics data, finding that 10.6 surpassed 10.5 in about five days. He notes that before Snow Leopard even went on sale, 11.2% of Mac-using DF visitors were already running it, reflecting the developer-heavy readership. He compares these numbers to similar analyses he conducted for Tiger and Panther releases. Gruber is careful to caveat that DF readers are not representative of Mac users in general, and he explains his methodology including filtering for Intel-only users since PowerPC Macs couldn't run Snow Leopard.
key_points: Snow Leopard surpassed Leopard among DF visitors in roughly five days after release
11.2% of Mac-using DF visitors were running Snow Leopard the day before it officially went on sale, reflecting the heavily developer-skewed audience
Weekend adoption blips suggest enthusiasts update home machines faster than IT departments update work machines
Mac users account for 77.5% of DF visitors, with Windows at 12.8% and iPhone OS at 7.5%
Among Windows-using DF visitors, XP still dominated at 71.2% versus Vista's 17.3%
money_quotes: Daring Fireball readers are, without question, not representative of Mac users in general.
By 27 August, the day before Snow Leopard went on sale, that number had grown to 11.2 percent. Even knowing how many developers read DF, that surprised me.
I suspect enthusiasts are quicker to update their home machines than IT departments are to update work machines.
So it took about five days for 10.6 to pass 10.5.
Mac users account for 77.5 percent of the total visitors to Daring Fireball; Windows clocks in at 12.8 percent and iPhone OS at 7.5.
key_insight: Snow Leopard's rapid adoption among DF's tech-savvy readership — with over 11% running it before it even went on sale — demonstrates both the enthusiasm of Apple's developer community and the limits of using any single site's analytics as a proxy for the broader market.
